assignment_description:
Introduction to this Course
⍞ Materials: Google Docs Learning Journal (you will create this today)

➜ INTRO
You will create a basic website or blog using Google Docs to access your prior knowledge and reflect on, record, and share what you are learning throughout this course.

➜ VIEW
∞ Link: Course Overview

➜ OPEN
∞ Link: My Learning Journal Template
• Click the blue "Use Template" button.
• Send the URL (link or website address) to your educator so they can see the updates you make as you go through the lessons.

You will use this as a learning journal throughout this first term, and you will respond within the journal you just created. Many lessons will begin and end with an opportunity to reflect on what you will learn or what was covered. This is your journal, and we encourage you to make it beautiful as you learn more about design throughout this course. Do not share any personal information. This will be referred to in future lessons as your "Google Docs Learning Journal."

➜ REFLECT
• Use the landing page (opening page) of your Google Docs Learning Journal to respond to the prompts under "What Stands Out to You About the Course Overview."
• Read the section "Goals for Term 1." Begin thinking about your philosophy of education by comparing the two statements that are provided and answer #3.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP:
Watch these videos if you are unfamiliar with Google Docs.
∞ Video Link: Using Google Docs to Create Web Pages
∞ Video Link: Dive deep into Using Google Docs

★ TEACHER NOTE
When you see instructions using this symbol ">", you are being given steps in order to navigate within the webpage or program/app.

assignment_description:
Introduction to HTML #1
⍞ Materials: your Google Docs Learning Journal

➜ INTRO
You will learn about the invention of HTML and its creator, Sir Tim Berners-Lee.

➜ OPEN
Your Google Docs Learning Journal and scroll down to the first lesson section called "Intro to HTML: Sir Tim Berners-Lee."

➜ THINK
Spend 5 minutes answering any of the following questions within your journal: (Anytime you do not know how to answer a question, write that! Starting by recognizing and recording what you do not know increases the possibility that you will feel good about learning and that the idea will stick in your mind.)
• What is the World Wide Web, and who invented it?
• What is the difference between the World Wide Web and the Internet?

➜ READ or WATCH
∞ Article Link: Sir Tim Berners-Lee (read)
∞ Video Link: Sir Tim Berners-Lee (video) (15 mins)
You will be expected to write a narration of this video, so pause the video and jot down notes when you notice something noteworthy.

➜ REFLECT
Complete your first entry in your Google Docs Learning Journal by spending 10 minutes writing a brief narration summarizing Berners-Lee’s journey from building devices for model trains to inventing HTML.

Introduction to HTML #2: Create a Technology Word Journal using HTML
⍞ Materials: your Google Docs Learning Journal, notepad on Windows or TextEdit on Mac, and Technology Word Journal

➜ OPEN
Open your Google Docs Learning Journal and scroll down to the second lesson section called "Intro to HTML."

➜ THINK
Spend 5 minutes answering the following question:
• How do websites know how to show words, pictures, and videos in the right places?

➜ WATCH (14 min)
∞ Video Link: Video #00
∞ Video Link: Video #01

➜ OPEN
In this section, you will either open NOTEPAD or TEXTEDIT, depending on which kind of computer you are using.
∞ Link: Alveary Technology 10 Course Word Journal
• Go to File> download> Plain Text (.txt) > open it in Notepad (PC) or TextEdit (Mac)

➜ FOLLOW DIRECTIONS
• Follow these directions for using NOTEPAD on a PC:
• Go to File> Save As > and save a working copy of the .txt file as backupwordjournal.txt > Save it another time with the extension .HTML instead of .txt.
• Follow these directions if you are using TEXTEDIT on a MAC:
• Hold down the Option key while choosing File, then Save As > type the name of the copy as backupwordjournal.txt and choose where to save it > save > Save it another time, holding down the Option key while choosing File, then Save As> type the name of the copy as backupwordjournal.HTML and choose where to save it > save >use .HTML.

➜ FOLLOW DIRECTIONS FOR PC AND MAC
• Now, close the copy you created and reopen it. It will open in your browser, and you will see that it has been rendered with the HTML formatting.
• Open the .txt document and examine how the HTML tags impacted the resulting webpage. Don't fret if you mess it up! Remember, you saved a backup.

➜ REFLECT
You will notice that entries for the terms "Technology" and "Computer" have been completed. Using these as an example, add an entry for one of the following terms to the new HTML Word Journal you just created in the Notebook or TextEdit program.
• HTML, HTML Tag, HTML element, or Markup Language

Introduction to HTML #3
⍞ Materials: your Google Docs Learning Journal and HTML Word Journal.

➜ INTRO
You will consider some given reasons for learning HTML and interact with HTML tags while receiving instant feedback.

➜ OPEN
Open your Google Docs Learning Journal and navigate to the next available lesson section.

➜ THINK
Spend 5 minutes answering any of the following questions:
• Have you ever right-clicked on a webpage and selected “View Page Source” or “Inspect”? Try it! What do you see?
• Why do you think all websites follow some basic structure rules to display content?
• Write about a website that surprised you by how "new" and "different" it looked.

➜ VIEW and PRACTICE
∞ Article Link: HTML Introduction by Geeks for Geeks
• Read the introduction.
• Scroll until you find the embedded "Basic HTML Code Example" and click on the triangle play button.
• Observe the output.
•Just for fun, insert "<marquee>" and "</marquee>" tags inside the opening header tag "<h1>" and the closing header tag "</h1>" as in the following example:
<h1><marquee>Welcome to My Webpage</marquee></h1>
• Click on the triangle play button and observe the output.

➜ REFLECT
While referring to the provided Geeks for Geeks HTML Introduction, spend 5-10 minutes completing your Google Docs Learning Journal answering the following questions:
• Of the five provided, which is the best reason for learning HTML?
• What does the "marquee" tag do?

➜ OPEN
Your working copy of the Word Journal.txt
• Create entries for 2 more of the following terms: HTML, HTML Element, HTML Tag, or Markup Language.
• If you are happy with the changes you have made to your HTML Word Journal so far, this might be a good time to consider updating the backup.

• TERMINOLOGY
∞ Article Link: A Simple Guide to HTML

Pioneers in the History of Technology: Alan Turing
⍞ Materials: your Google Docs Learning Journal and Google Slides Technology Timeline

➜ INTRO
Alan Turing (1912–1954) was an English mathematician, logician, and computer scientist often considered the father of modern computing. During World War II, Turing played a crucial role and significantly aided the Allied war effort. His work laid the foundation for artificial intelligence and modern computer science. Post-war, his contributions continued to influence the emerging field of computer science.

➜ THINK
Use the next lesson section of your Google Docs Learning Journal to answer the following question:
What impact do you think computers have had on how wars are conducted and won?

➜ READ
∞ Article Link: Alan Turing: The codebreaker who saved 'millions of lives'

➜ OPEN
Open your Google Slides Technology Timeline.
• Navigate to the section corresponding to the relevant date and create an entry about Alan Turing.

➜ REFLECT
Continue your Google Docs Learning Journal entry, answering at least two of the following questions and justifying your answers:
• Why do you think Alan Turing’s work was so significant during WWII?
• How do you see his contributions reflected in the technology we use today?
• What ethical considerations should we keep in mind as technology continues to advance?

Graphic Designers: Paul Rand
⍞ Materials: your Google Docs Learning Journal and Google Slides Technology Timeline

➜ INTRO
Now that you know a little about graphic design and have had an introduction to creating designs in Canva, I want to introduce you to an influential American graphic designer, best known for creating iconic logos for brands such as IBM, ABC, and UPS: Paul Rand. He pioneered modern corporate design by combining simplicity with creative vision, emphasizing the power of images to communicate a brand’s essence. His work greatly influenced visual communication, advocating for design that was both functional and aesthetically pleasing. Rand believed in the importance of clear, simple designs to communicate complex ideas effectively.

➜ THINK
∞ Image Link: IBM, ABC, and UPS logos
Use the next lesson section of your Google Docs Learning Journal to answer the following question.
• Observe the IBM, ABC, and UPS logos. What do you notice?

➜ WATCH

Graphic Designers: Saul Bass
⍞ Materials: your Google Docs Learning Journal and Google Slides Technology Timeline

➜ INTRO
Now that you have learned about Paul Rand and had an introduction to creating logos and book covers in Canva, I want to introduce you to Saul Bass, who was a groundbreaking graphic designer and filmmaker, renowned for his work in movie posters and title sequences. His minimalist style transformed film graphics, making them integral to storytelling. Famous works include the logos for AT&T and the Academy Awards, as well as iconic opening sequences for movies like Psycho and Anatomy of a Murder. Bass’s innovative approach combined art and design, creating visual masterpieces that conveyed deep meaning with simplicity.

➜ THINK
Use the next lesson section of your Google Docs Learning Journal to answer the following question: In your opinion, how might designing a movie poster be similar to designing a book cover, and how might it be different?

➜ WATCH

➜ REFLECT

· Do you remember learning about the invention of HTML and its creator, Sir Tim Berners-Lee, at the start of this term? Open your Google Slides Technology Timeline; navigate to the section corresponding to the relevant date, and create an entry about him.

· Using the Canva infographic maker, create an infographic about HTML for print and another one for sharing online. 
1. Using your pencil and paper, brainstorm everything you know about HTML.  
2. Things to consider when planning your infographic: HTML facts and basic tags, describing what basic tags do, how you will use the seven fundamental elements of design, what fonts, colors, and images you will use, and how it will be laid out.
3. ∞ Quick Link: Canva Infographic Maker

· Share the media you created this term that best demonstrates the concepts we covered. 
1. Demonstrate how you intentionally considered the concepts of line, shape, color, texture, space, value, and form
2. Explain your reason for your choice of fonts
3. Describe what color scheme is employed and how it is balanced.
4. Explain your intention with the layout.

Self-Assessment
⍞ Materials: your Google Docs Learning Journal and HTML Word Journal

➜ INTRO
You will create your final entry in your Google Docs Learning Journal and reflect on your experience using this tool before exploring Adobe Express and using it to create a Webpage to use as a learning journal throughout this second term.

➜ THINK
Create your final entry in your Google Docs Learning Journal and answer the following question:
• What are 3 pros and 2 cons of using Google Docs for creating a learning journal or a weblog (aka blog)?

➜ WATCH:
∞ Video Link: Create an Adobe Express Webpage (12 mins)
• You will be expected to write about what you watch in this course, so pause the video and jot down notes when you notice something noteworthy.

➜ OPEN
∞ Link: Adobe Express Online Journal Maker
• Create an Adobe Express Webpage to use as a learning journal throughout this term. Just like last term, each lesson will start with a “THINK” prompt; you will respond using this new Adobe Express journal instead of your Google Docs Learning Journal. Many lessons will end with an opportunity to reflect on what was covered. This is your journal; we encourage you to make it beautiful as you learn more about design throughout this course. Do not share any personal information. Send the URL (link or website address) to your educator so that they can see the updates you make as you go through the lessons.

➜ REFLECT
• Use your new Adobe Express Journal to review what you have learned and completed over the first term of this course, or just write about what you found most interesting or beautiful, and then write about which concepts you would like to learn more about during Term 2.

Can you think of how your device could use this tool?

Digital Photography: Composition
⍞ Materials: Adobe Express Learning Journal and a mobile device with a camera, or a DSLR (optional)

➜ INTRO
The video that introduced you to the elements of design in term one mentioned the rule of thirds; many teachers suggest practising using the rule of thirds until it becomes a habit, then you can learn when it is meaningful to break the rule. Two other common pieces of advice are as follows: (1) get low and (2) get in close.

➜ THINK
Using your Adobe Express Learning Journal spend 5 minutes considering the following pieces of photo composition advice:
• Which of the suggestions do you resonate with most? Why? Why do you think these rules are so popular?

➜ WATCH
You will view a video on a composition strategy.
∞ Video Link: Sub-Framing In Visual Composition (9 mins)
• You will be expected to write about what you watch in this course, so pause the video and jot down notes when you notice something noteworthy.

➜ MAKE
• Try to make some photographs using the rule of thirds and sub-frames. When using sub-frames, make some from the floor and from very close up to the subject. Then, make some photographs by very intentionally going against one of these pieces of advice.

➜ REFLECT
Complete your Adobe Express Learning Journal entry for today by spending 5 minutes considering the following questions:
• Which of your photographs ended up looking the best or most interesting? Why?
• What piece of advice were you being intentional about using or eschewing when you took that photograph?

Digital Photography: Composition Project
⍞ Materials: Adobe Express Learning Journal and a mobile device with a camera, or a DSLR (optional)

➜ INTRO
You may have noticed that we were saying "make photographs" in the previous lesson instead of the more common expression "take photographs." That language is intentional.

➜ THINK
Using your Adobe Express Learning Journal, spend 5 minutes considering why photographers might choose to say they make photographs rather than take photographs, and answer the following questions:
• How can art be judged as good art or otherwise?
• Does context and the artist's intention matter?

➜ PRACTICE
After reflecting on what you learned from your last photography session, pick a subject for today's project and spend around 20 minutes photographing it in a variety of ways. Make some photographs using the rule of thirds and sub-frames. You can use sub-frames from the floor and from very close up to the subject. If you break a rule, do it intentionally.

➜ REFLECT
Complete your Adobe Express Learning Journal entry for today by spending 5 minutes considering the following questions:
• Which of your photographs you took today ended up looking the best or most interesting? Why?
• What piece of advice were you being intentional about using or eschewing when you took that photograph?

CSS: Introduction - Selectors, Properties, and Values
⍞ Materials: your Carrd Learning Journal and your HTML Word Journal

➜ THINK
You might remember coming across the term "CSS" in lesson 6 of Term 1. Using your Carrd Learning Journal, spend 5 minutes answering the following questions:
• Imagine you’re designing a website about something you love. What would you want it to look like? Would it be bright and colorful or simple and clean? How would you want people to feel when they see it? What kinds of choices would you need to make to create that feeling—colors, text styles, shapes, or how things are arranged?

CSS: Introduction - Simple Selectors, Adding CSS to HTML, and Commenting
⍞ Materials: your Carrd Learning Journal and Google Slides Technology Timeline

➜ INTRO
HTML wasn't meant to format a web page just to describe the content: egs <h1>heading</h1>, <p>paragraph</p>. When font and color tags were later added, it caused a lot of problems. That's why the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) created CSS. CSS took the style formatting away from the HTML page! The style can be saved in an external .css file, so the look of an entire website can be changed all at once.

➜ THINK
Using your Carrd Learning Journal, spend 5 minutes answering the following questions:
• What do you know about the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)?
• Who do you think they are?

➜ WATCH
There are three videos that you will watch from the CSS Tutorial by w3schools.com.

∞ Video Link: Simple Selectors (>2 mins)
• Make note of Element Selector, ID Selector, and Class Selector.

∞ Video Link: How to add CSS to HTML (>2 mins)
• Make note of External CSS, Internal CSS, and Inline CSS.

∞ Video Link: Comments (2 mins)
• You are expected to write about what you watch in this course, so pause the video and jot down notes when you notice something noteworthy.

➜ BROWSE & EXPLORE
∞ Link: Group Selectors

➜ REFLECT
Using your Carrd Learning Journal, spend 5 minutes considering the following question:
If CSS comments don't show up or change anything on a webpage, why is it important to know about them?

• EXTRA HELPING
If you want to learn more about the W3C:
∞ Video Link: W3C: The World Wide Web Consortium

Be sure to add an entry for W3C to your Google Slides Technology Timeline.

Broadcasting: Introduction to Audacity
⍞ Materials: your Carrd Learning Journal, your HTML Word Journal, and a phone or a USB microphone with a pop filter (optional)

➜ INTRO
Have you ever had an idea, a story, or a message you wished more people could hear? Throughout history, broadcasting—sharing information with a wide audience—has been a powerful way to shape ideas, entertain, and connect people across great distances. From early radio to modern streaming services, broadcasting has changed the way we communicate. Podcasting is a modern form of broadcasting. Unlike traditional radio, where programs are scheduled at set times, podcasts let people record and share audio content that can be listened to anytime, anywhere. Whether it’s storytelling, interviews, news, or discussions on a favorite topic, podcasting gives anyone the ability to broadcast their voice to the world.

➜ THINK
Using your Carrd Learning Journal, spend 5 minutes answering the following question:
• If you had a microphone and anyone in the whole world could listen whenever they wanted to, what would you say?

➜ WATCH
∞ Video: Master Audio Editing Basics (9 mins)
Stop and consider as you watch. Add at least 1 new term you come across to your HTML Word Journal.

➜ DOWNLOAD
∞ Link: Audacity

➜ MAKE
Using Audacity, record yourself reciting a poem, a Psalm, or something else of your choosing. If you don't have a microphone, you can record it using a phone's memo app, email it to yourself, download it on your computer, and import it. Additionally, you can also download and import some sound effects and music from Pixabay.
∞ Link: Pixabay Sounds
∞ Link: Pixabay Music

➜ REFLECT
Use your Carrd Journal to reflect for 5 minutes on what you are learning and record any questions you have on the topic of Audacity and audio engineering.

Broadcasting: The History of Radio Podcast Pre-production Phase
⍞ Materials: Audacity, your Carrd Learning Journal, and pencil/paper or word processing app.

➜ THINK
Using your Carrd Learning Journal, spend a minute answering the following question:
Do you listen to podcasts? Why or why not?

➜ READ
You will be creating a podcast on the history of radio. Today you will write the script, and next time you will record it. First, make a list of points you might use as you browse the following sources for the content:
∞ Link: Who Invented Radio?
∞ Link: Guglielmo Marconi
∞ Link: A Short History of Radio
∞ Link: 1890s – 1930s: Radio

➜ PRE-PRODUCTION PHASE
Plan and write your script following a clear broadcasting structure to engage your audience: Hook, Introduction, Main Content, and Conclusion.
• Hook (First 2–3 Sentences) Start with a question, a surprising fact, or a bold statement to grab your listener’s attention. Example: "Imagine turning on the radio and hearing, ‘This is not a drill! Martians have landed in New Jersey!’ In 1938, Orson Welles’ War of the Worlds did just that—and today, I'm talking about the power of radio to captivate an audience.”

• Introduction: Introduce yourself and your topic. Example: "Welcome to my podcast! I’m [your name], and today, I'm exploring how radio revolutionized communication. From war updates to sports broadcasts, radio shaped history—and we can learn a lot from its past."

• Main Content: Share your key points in a clear and engaging way. Use a storytelling approach to keep listeners engaged. Example: "Before TV and the internet, families gathered around the radio for news, drama, and entertainment. When radio first broadcast the Hindenburg disaster in 1937, listeners felt like they were right there as the reporter cried out, ‘Oh, the humanity!’ It proved how powerful broadcasting could be."

• Conclusion and Call to Action: Wrap up your main idea with a strong closing statement. Example: "From news to entertainment, radio shaped the world. But today, podcasting gives everyone the power to broadcast their voice. What story will you tell? Thanks for listening!"

Broadcasting: The History of Radio Podcast Production Phase
⍞ Materials: Audacity, and your history of radio script

➜ PRODUCTION PHASE
Read the first section or sentence. Press record and say what you just read into the mic or phone without reading it. Speak in an animated voice with enthusiasm, but slowly and clearly. Repeat this till every section or sentence is recorded. Make sure the meter isn't clipping or peaking. If it is, your audio will sound distorted. You will need to turn down your mic a little.
• If time allows, rerecord any sections that could be more beautiful, but accept that it will not be perfect.

Broadcasting: Topic of Personal Interest Project Pre-Production Phase
⍞ Materials: your Carrd Learning Journal and a pencil/paper or word processing app.

➜ THINK
Using your Carrd Learning Journal, spend 5 minutes listing the ideas you have encountered so far in this course or other recent courses.
• Which ones came easiest to mind?
• Which ideas interest you most? Why?

➜ PREPRODUCTION
Choose a topic we've explored that has sparked your curiosity, challenged your thinking, or excited you. This could be a concept, a project, a breakthrough moment, or something that connected to your life in a meaningful way. Spend about 25 minutes planning and writing your script. Focus on clarity, energy, and engaging your audience.

Use the following structure to write a script for your next broadcasting project:
• Hook (First 2–3 Sentences): Start with a question, a surprising fact, or a bold statement to grab your listener’s attention.
Example: “Did you know that the internet runs on a hidden system of underwater cables? Today, we're diving into how they work and why they matter.”

• Introduction (About 30 Seconds): Introduce yourself and your topic. Explain why this topic is interesting or important.
Example: “Welcome to my podcast! I’m [your name], and today I want to talk about the most surprising thing I've learned in this course: how artificial intelligence is shaping the future of creativity.”

• Main Content (1–2 Minutes): Share your key points in a clear and engaging way. Use a mix of facts, examples, or personal insights to make your message compelling.
If relevant, use a storytelling approach to keep listeners engaged.
Example: “At first, I thought coding was just about numbers and logic, but then I discovered how it can be used to create art, music, and even games.”

• Conclusion and Call to Action (Last 30 Seconds): Wrap up your main idea with a strong closing statement. Consider leaving your audience with a thought-provoking question or challenge.
Example: “So, what do you think? Will AI replace human creativity, or will it help us become more creative than ever? Think about it, and thanks for listening!”

∞ Link: 1986: Children Imagine Life in 2020
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Technology — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· You will interview two people, someone older than you and someone younger than you. Ask them about their technological concerns right now and their predictions about future technology. Before you do that, watch the video and jot down a few answers to the "Think" questions.
1. Video Quick Link: 1986: Children Imagine Life in 2020 (3 mins)
2. Think: Using your Carrd Journal, consider the following questions: What are people's biggest technological concerns? How are those concerns similar or different from the concerns of the people from 50 or 60 years ago?  How are those concerns similar or different from the concerns people will have in 50 or 60 years from now? 
3. Interview: Choose the media or method for presenting their predictions and plan or script your project. Rather than think about your favorite approach, consider which media format will best serve the content. Would the ideas be best communicated through an animation, blog, slideshow, an infographic, or an interactive app? Consider using the storytelling structure we have used, regardless of the format you choose to use.

· Spend 5 minutes using your Carrd Journal to answer the following questions:
1. Who did you interview? 
2. Which media or method for presenting their predictions did you choose? 
3. Did you use the storytelling structure to plan or script your project? Why or why not?

· Produce your final media production for the course and share it with your teacher.
